§ About

  • ColophonCredits
¶ Hello

I’m Brian Hinton, accessibility lead at Superhuman (formerly known as Grammarly). I spend most of my time thinking about what happens when you treat design as a form of care — for the people using the software, for the engineers building it, and for the systems that hold it all together.

For years my online presence was rented space — social profiles and digital CVs on platforms I didn’t own. This site is me building a home on the internet instead. The foundation is my own, the creaky floorboards are ones I coded, and the front door is always open.

It’s a place to slow down and share ideas that invite reflection rather than consumption — on design systems, accessibility, leadership, and whatever I’m paying attention to now.

Read the essays See what I’m up to now
on the desk, april ’26 →
§ Writing

An essay on screen-reader announcements as copywriting.

§ Building

Prism — a perceptual contrast checker. Alpha with four teams.

§ Reading

The Craftsman, second pass. Design Justice, slowly.

See the now page →
§ Essays

Recent writing

§ Essay 12 April 2026 · 9 min

The focus ring is a social contract

When you remove a focus ring, you are not tidying the design — you are telling keyboard users they are not your audience. Here is how I stopped apologising for them.

A11y Keyboard
§ Essay 28 March 2026 · 12 min

Slow accessibility

Six years ago I was shipping a11y fixes in week-long sprints and calling it done. I was wrong. A note on working at the pace the work actually needs.

A11y Practice
§ Essay 04 March 2026 · 11 min

Managing specialists when you were one

The hardest part of moving from senior designer to manager of designers is that the skill that got you here will, gently, start to get in the way.

Management Craft
¶ Notes

From the notebook

  • Apr 14

    Watched a colleague navigate our app with VoiceOver this morning. They reached the compose button in 19 tab stops. Our target was 4. There is a month of work in that gap.

    office, tue
  • Apr 11

    Rereading The Design of Everyday Things. Norman is still right about almost everything, and still wrong about the same few things. I think the wrong bits are the most useful.

    train home
  • Apr 07

    Tiny win: we landed focus-visible across the product tonight. 214 components. One PR. Twelve months of groundwork.

    midnight
  • Apr 02

    Mentorship conversation with J — she asked how to know when to stop investigating and start shipping. I said: when the next investigation would only rearrange the doubt, not reduce it.

    weekly 1:1
§ Projects

Currently building

P
Contrast tool
§ In progress 2026

Prism

An open-source contrast checker that accounts for ambient lighting, display calibration, and the viewer's own perception. Currently in preview.
Q
Pattern lib
§ Maintained 2025

Quiet Keys

A keyboard-shortcut design pattern library. 40+ documented interactions with rationale, screen-reader behaviour, and escape hatches.
§ Reading

On the shelf, this month

The Craftsman

The Craftsman

Richard Sennett

Second read. The chapter on the hand is doing most of the work.

Design Justice

Design Justice

Sasha Costanza-Chock

Slow read — one chapter a week, with notes.

A Web for Everyone

A Web for Everyone

Horton & Quesenbery

Rereading for a workshop.

§ Now

What I’m up to, this month

Writing an essay on announcement copy. Building Prism, a perceptual contrast checker now alpha with four teams. Managing three designers and one a11y engineer at Superhuman. Avoiding new conference talks through the summer — writing instead.

§ About

Hello.

I’ve spent about equal time as a designer, a developer, and a manager of teams that include both. The longer I do this, the less those three labels feel like separate jobs — they feel like three rooms in the same house, with doors between them that I keep propped open.

Design with Thought

A writing-and-projects site by Brian Hinton. Essays arrive slowly. Notes, more often.
